tasks.sourceTextEn: Leo had never won anything in his life. While his older brother filled the living room shelf with shining medals from football, chess, and swimming, Leo's only contribution was a single dusty certificate for «most improved» in primary school, its corners curling with age. He had grown used to being the quiet one, the boy who clapped for others and slipped to the back of every photograph. So when the town announced a charity bake-off to raise money for the children's ward, he surprised everyone, including himself, by signing up. His grandmother had taught him to bake when he was small, balancing him on a wooden chair beside the warm oven, guiding his clumsy fingers as they folded flour into butter. That was before her hands grew too weak to hold a wooden spoon, before her voice faded to a whisper, yet somehow the smell of warm bread still felt exactly like her hug. For three weeks Leo practised every single evening, burning loaves until they turned to charcoal, watching cakes collapse sadly in the middle, and once setting off the smoke alarm at midnight so loudly that the neighbour's dog howled. His mother only laughed and helped him scrape the pans, never once telling him to stop. On the morning of the bake-off, he carried his carefully wrapped honey cake into the crowded community hall, his heart thudding so hard against his ribs that he was sure others could hear it. The other contestants looked like seasoned professionals, with crisp white aprons and trays of glossy, perfectly even pastries arranged like jewels. Leo suddenly felt foolish standing there with his slightly lopsided cake and its uneven golden glaze. Then he noticed an elderly woman sitting alone near the judges' table, her silver hair pinned neatly back, and his chest tightened, because for one strange, breathless second she reminded him of his grandmother. He set his cake down and waited, unsure whether he had made a terrible mistake by coming at all.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The rain had been falling steadily for three days, drumming on rooftops and turning the lanes to rivers of mud, when Nina found the injured bird beneath the old oak tree near her house. It was a small sparrow, no bigger than her palm, one wing bent at an unnatural angle, its tiny chest rising and falling far too quickly. Nina was twelve and had always been afraid of touching wild animals, frightened of their sharp beaks and the wild, unknowable way they moved, but something about the bird's frightened, glassy eyes made her forget her fear entirely. She knelt in the cold mud and wrapped it gently in her woollen scarf, and carried it home cradled against her chest, rainwater dripping steadily from her hair and soaking through her sleeves. Her father, a busy hospital doctor who rarely had time for such things, sighed heavily when he saw the soaked, shivering bundle in her arms. «We can't save every creature, Nina,» he said quietly, not unkindly, his tired eyes meeting hers. But Nina refused to give up. She made a small box lined with the softest cloth she could find, fed the bird single drops of water from the tip of a spoon, and stayed up late into the night watching over it by the glow of her bedside lamp. Each morning she rushed downstairs before breakfast, her heart in her throat, to check whether it had survived another night. Day by day the bird grew slowly stronger, hopping a little, blinking up at her, though its wing remained stubbornly crooked. Nina began to understand that one day, perhaps soon, she would have to decide whether to keep it safe forever in its little box, or to open the window and let it try the wide, dangerous, open sky. The thought filled her with both a soaring hope and a strange, aching sadness she could not put into words.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Daniel had been looking forward to the school camping trip for months, counting down the days on the calendar above his bed. He had packed his bag twice, checked the weather forecast three times, and even bought a new flashlight with his own carefully saved pocket money, testing its bright beam against his bedroom wall each night. But on the day before the trip, his father came home pale and quiet, having lost his job, and the joyful mood at home disappeared overnight like a candle pinched out. That evening Daniel overheard his parents talking in low, worried voices in the kitchen, about money, about unpaid bills, about how they would possibly manage through the coming months. The forty dollars for the trip, which had seemed so ordinary a week ago, suddenly felt like an enormous, impossible sum. Daniel lay awake long into the night, staring at his packed bag waiting hopefully in the corner of his room. In the grey morning light, without telling anyone, he quietly unpacked everything, folding each shirt back into the drawer, and pushed the empty bag deep into the back of his closet. When his mother asked if he was excited, summoning a tired smile, he forced a smile of his own and said he had decided he did not really want to go after all. She looked at him for a long, searching moment, as if she could see straight through his careful lie to the truth beneath, but she said nothing, only touched his shoulder gently. At school, his friends talked endlessly about tents and crackling campfires and the games they would play under the stars. Daniel nodded along, pretending to share their excitement, while a heavy stone settled deeper in his chest with every passing word. He told himself firmly that this was the right thing to do, the grown-up thing, even though it hurt far more than he had ever expected.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Sophie was the new girl at Riverside High, and being new in the middle of the school year felt like wearing an invisible sign that said «look at me» wherever she went. She had moved clear across the country because of her mother's new job, leaving behind every friend she had ever known, the bedroom she had grown up in, and the streets she could walk with her eyes closed. During lunch she sat alone at the far end of a long table, pretending to read the same page of a book over and over so she would not look quite so painfully lonely. The other students seemed to already belong to invisible groups, sealed circles of laughter and inside jokes she felt she could never enter. On her third day, while the corridors churned with the usual rush between lessons, she noticed a girl named Priya struggling to carry a tall, swaying stack of art supplies down the crowded hallway. A few students laughed unkindly as one box wobbled dangerously near the top, but nobody stepped forward to help. Sophie hesitated, her old fear of being noticed rising up, remembering just how invisible she herself felt, and then she hurried over and caught the box just before it tipped and crashed to the floor. Priya looked up, startled, and gave her a grateful, slightly embarrassed smile that crinkled the corners of her eyes. Together they walked the rest of the way to the art room, talking quietly and easily about paints and pencils and the mural Priya was helping to design. For the first time since she had arrived in this strange new town, Sophie felt the cold, tight knot of loneliness in her stomach begin, ever so slightly, to loosen. She did not yet know, as she breathed in the comforting smell of turpentine and clay, that this one small moment of kindness would change far more than just her lunchtime seating arrangement.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Grandpa Wei had grown vegetables in the same small garden for over forty years, his hands knowing every inch of its dark, rich soil. Every spring, the neighbourhood children would gather at his low wooden fence to watch him press seeds into the earth, and every autumn he would share his plump tomatoes and crisp green beans with anyone who happened to pass by. But this year was different, and a shadow had fallen over the little plot. A property developer had bought up all the surrounding land, and Grandpa Wei's beloved garden now stood directly in the way of a planned new car park. A cold, official letter had arrived, stating plainly that the garden would be cleared at the end of the month. When his granddaughter Lily visited that weekend, she found him sitting quietly on his old wooden stool among the rows, staring at the tender young green shoots he had planted only weeks before, his weathered hands resting still in his lap. He did not complain, for that was not his way, but Lily could see a deep, quiet sadness settled in the lines of his face. «Things change, little one,» he said softly, patting the dark soil as though comforting an old and faithful friend. Lily, who was usually so shy that she rarely spoke up even when she should, felt a fierce, surprising protest rising hotly inside her chest. That garden was not just dirt and plants to be scraped away; it was forty years of patience and early mornings, of generosity freely given and simple, shared joy. As she walked home alone that evening, the setting sun painting the rooftops gold, an idea began to take shape in her mind, small and uncertain at first but growing steadily and stubbornly with every step, like one of Grandpa Wei's own seeds quietly reaching up through the dark toward the light.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Marcus had always been the fastest runner on the school track team, and everyone, including himself, simply expected him to win the regional championship without much trouble. Training came easily to him; his long legs ate up the track effortlessly, and he rarely had to push himself the way his panting, red-faced teammates did. His coach, a wiry man with sharp eyes, often warned him that natural talent alone would not always be enough, that one day someone hungrier would come along, but Marcus only smiled, shrugged, and jogged another lazy lap. Then, just six weeks before the big race, a quieter boy named Sam joined the team. Sam was not especially fast, and his form was rough at first, but he trained with a fierce, steady discipline that Marcus had never seen in anyone before. He arrived first every single morning while the grass was still wet with dew, and left last every evening as the sky turned purple, running the same brutal hills again and again until his legs visibly trembled beneath him. At first Marcus found it almost amusing, this stubborn boy chasing a talent he simply did not have. But week by week, Sam's times steadily improved, the gap closing inch by inch, while Marcus, leaning lazily on his old habits, stayed exactly the same. During a practice race one grey, rainy afternoon, Sam finished only half a second behind him, and Marcus felt a cold, unfamiliar flicker of worry for the very first time in his life. That night, lying awake in bed and listening to the rain on the glass, he replayed the race over and over in his mind. He realised, with an uncomfortable, sinking honesty, that he had been coasting for years, never once truly testing his own limits. The championship was approaching fast, and for the first time ever, Marcus was no longer sure that he would win.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Every Saturday morning, twelve-year-old Amara visited the small public library at the corner of her street, a ritual as certain as the sunrise. It was an old building with creaky wooden floors that announced every footstep and the warm, comforting smell of dusty pages and old leather, run by a kind librarian named Mrs Owen who knew every single regular visitor by name and remembered exactly which books they loved. For Amara, the library was far more than just a place to borrow books; it was a quiet, golden refuge from her noisy, crowded home, where four younger siblings squabbled in two small rooms and she could never find a moment of peace. So when she saw the notice taped crookedly to the front door one chilly grey morning, her heart sank like a stone. The library was to close for good at the end of the year, because the city could no longer afford to keep its doors open. Mrs Owen tried bravely to smile as she explained, but her eyes were rimmed with red and her voice wavered. «People can use the big library downtown,» she said gently, though everyone standing there knew it was a full hour away by two crowded buses. That afternoon, Amara sat quietly among the shelves she loved so dearly, running her fingers slowly along the worn, familiar spines of her favourite books, as if saying goodbye. She thought about all the other children who, like her, had no quiet place in all the world to read or dream or simply breathe. An ordinary girl could not possibly save a whole library, she told herself firmly, trying to crush the hope rising in her chest. And yet, as she looked across the room at the hopeful, bent faces of the younger children doing their homework at the scratched wooden tables, she felt with sudden certainty that she had to at least try.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Tom had spent his whole summer building a model sailboat for the city's annual pond regatta, the long warm afternoons slipping away unnoticed as he worked. In his quiet garage, surrounded by the smell of sawdust and glue, he had carefully shaped the wooden hull with sandpaper until it was smooth as river stone, sewn tiny white sails from a scrap of his mother's old cloth, and painted the little boat a deep, hopeful blue. He named it Persistence, after a word his late father had often used, written in tiny letters along the side. Tom's father had loved sailing more than almost anything, and although he had died two years earlier, leaving a silence in the house that never quite filled, Tom still felt closest to him when he worked patiently with his hands the way his father once had. On the bright morning of the regatta, Tom carried the boat carefully to the park, where dozens of children and their families had gathered in a cheerful crowd around the wide, glittering pond. Beside the others, his boat looked smaller and plainer than the shiny, expensive store-bought models lined up along the water's edge. A few older boys snickered behind their hands when they saw the slightly uneven stitching of his sails, and Tom's cheeks burned hot with embarrassment. But he gripped the little blue boat tightly and reminded himself, fiercely, exactly why he had built it and whose memory it carried. When the whistle blew, shrill and clear across the water, all the boats were released into the pond at once in a colourful jumble. A sudden, sharp gust of wind swept across the surface, scattering the fleet in every direction and tipping two boats clean over. Tom watched, holding his breath, his heart pounding, as his small blue boat caught the breeze square in its sails and began, slowly and steadily, to move ahead of all the others.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Hannah had been chosen to play the lead role in the school's winter play, and at first she had been absolutely thrilled, floating through the corridors on a cloud of pride. But as opening night drew steadily closer, a new and deeply unwelcome feeling crept into her chest: stage fright, cold and heavy as a stone. During rehearsals her voice shook and cracked, her hands trembled so badly she had to clasp them behind her back, and twice she forgot her lines completely, her mind going as blank and white as a fresh sheet of paper while everyone waited. Her drama teacher, Ms Carter, was endlessly patient and encouraging, offering gentle smiles and kind words, but Hannah could see the worry flickering in the other students' eyes whenever she stumbled. The girl who had been chosen as her understudy, a quiet sophomore named Beth, knew every single line perfectly and never seemed the slightest bit nervous. Part of Hannah secretly, shamefully wished she could simply hand the role over to Beth and disappear forever into the safe, dark anonymity of the audience. On the afternoon before the performance, Hannah sat entirely alone in the empty auditorium, staring at the dark, silent stage where a single work light cast long shadows. She thought about all the long hours of practice, the beautiful costume her mother had stayed up late sewing by hand stitch by careful stitch, and the way her little sister had proudly told everyone at school that her big sister Hannah was going to be a real star. She did not want to let any of them down, not for anything in the world, but the fear felt larger and heavier than all of them combined, pressing the breath from her lungs. Somehow, before the heavy red curtain rose tomorrow night, she would have to find a way to face it.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Jake and his grandfather had never been close. Jake lived in the bustling city and visited the old farm only once or twice a year, usually staying glued to the glowing screen of his phone while his grandfather worked silently and patiently in the distant fields. This summer, however, his parents had sent him to stay for a whole long month, hoping the fresh country air and open skies would do him some good. Jake was not at all pleased about it. The farm had no fast internet, no shopping centres, no friends, and nothing, he thought gloomily, to do at all from dawn until dusk. His grandfather, a weathered man of very few words, woke before the sun rose each day to milk the cows and tend the crops, his movements slow and sure, never once complaining about the endless, back-breaking work. For the entire first week, the two of them barely spoke beyond a few necessary words passed across the table at mealtimes, an awkward silence stretching between them like a fence. Then one wild, stormy night, when the wind howled and rain lashed the windows, a rotten section of the old fence collapsed, and several frightened sheep wandered out into the dark, rain-soaked hills. His grandfather, breathing heavily and pressing a hand to his side, admitted in a low voice that his back was simply too sore to chase them down alone. For the first time ever, he looked at Jake with something like a question in his tired, ageing eyes. Jake stood frozen for a long moment in the open doorway, the cold rain blowing in around his ankles, his heart suddenly hammering. Then, without quite knowing why, without stopping to think it through, he reached for his boots and a flashlight and stepped out into the roaring storm beside the old man.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Ella had a secret she had kept from everyone at school: she could barely read. She was fourteen, clever in many ways, quick with numbers and brilliant at drawing, the kind of girl who could sketch a teacher's face in the margin of a worksheet so perfectly that her friends would gasp. Yet the letters on a page seemed to twist and dance whenever she tried to make sense of them, sliding around like restless fish she could never quite catch. For years she had hidden her struggle behind clever excuses, copying answers from friends during silent reading, pretending she had forgotten her glasses, or claiming, with a casual shrug, that she had already finished the book at home in bed. Each excuse cost her a little piece of herself, and the fear of being discovered followed her everywhere like a cold shadow that never left her side, even in her dreams. She had grown used to the constant ache of hiding, to the way her stomach knotted whenever a teacher's eyes scanned the room for a volunteer. Then a new English teacher, Mr Bowen, arrived at the school, a soft-spoken man with kind, watchful eyes. Unlike the others, he seemed to notice the small ways Ella avoided reading aloud, the way she stared too hard at the page as though willing the words to behave, the brief flash of panic that crossed her face whenever he asked a question. One grey afternoon, as the others packed away their books, he quietly asked her to stay behind. Ella's heart raced, and she imagined the worst: humiliation, punishment, the whole school whispering her secret in the corridors. But Mr Bowen did not scold her. Instead, he sat down beside her, slid a thin book gently across the desk, and asked, in a calm and patient voice, whether reading had ever felt difficult for her. For the first time in years, Ella felt the heavy weight of her secret begin to crack open, and she did not know whether to feel terrified or relieved.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The school science fair was only two days away, and Ravi's project was a disaster. He had spent weeks building a model of a working water filter, hoping to show how dirty water could be made clean using nothing more than simple, cheap materials that any family could find. It mattered to him more than he could explain, because his cousins in his parents' home village often had no safe water to drink, and he still remembered the summer his youngest cousin had fallen ill after drinking from the muddy river. Every time he pictured that, his determination burned brighter. But every time he tested his filter, the water trickled out almost as murky as before, leaving a brownish stain on the white cloth beneath. Frustration gnawed at him until his shoulders ached with tension. His partner, a cheerful but easily distracted boy named Owen, had spent far more time playing games on his phone than helping, and now Ravi felt completely alone with the broken project, surrounded by his own failure. That evening, he sat at the kitchen table heaped with tubes, sand, charcoal, and crumpled notes covered in crossed-out diagrams, ready to give up entirely. His mother, passing by with a basket of warm laundry, paused and looked at the hopeless mess and then at her son's discouraged, downcast face. She did not understand the science, the layers of gravel or the chemistry of charcoal, but she understood her boy. «Sometimes,» she said gently, resting a hand on his shoulder, «when something does not work, the problem is not the whole idea, only one small part of it.» Then she carried the laundry away, humming softly. Ravi sat very still and stared at his filter for a long time after she left the room, turning her words over and over in his mind. Slowly, almost reluctantly, an idea began to form, and he reached again for the materials he had nearly thrown away.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Mei had practised the piano every single day for six years, dreaming of the day she would perform in the city's youth concert. The recital was the biggest event of her musical life, and her teacher had chosen her, out of all the students, to play a difficult piece that few children her age could ever hope to manage. For weeks she rehearsed until her fingers ached and the keys seemed to leave grooves in her fingertips, determined to make her parents proud, the parents who had quietly given up new clothes and small holidays so that she could keep her lessons. Music was their gift to her, and this performance was meant to be her gift back to them. But two days before the concert, hurrying down the icy school steps, Mei slipped and fell hard, sprawling onto the frozen stone. The doctor wrapped her right wrist firmly and told her, kindly but clearly, that she must not play for at least a week. Mei stared at the white bandage in disbelief, feeling her dream slip away through her fingers like water. That night she cried quietly into her pillow, certain that all those years of patient practice had come to nothing, that she had failed everyone who believed in her. The next morning, however, her teacher came to visit, stamping snow from her boots at the door. Instead of looking disappointed, she sat beside Mei on the edge of the bed and spoke softly about something Mei had never once considered. There were other ways to share music, she said, her voice warm and certain, other roles in a concert besides being the one whose fingers touched the keys. Mei wiped her eyes with the back of her good hand and listened, hardly daring to breathe, and a fragile, unexpected hope began to flicker quietly inside her chest.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Carlos had moved to a new country with his family only three months earlier, and the language still felt like a locked door he could not open, no matter how hard he pushed. At school, words rushed past him too quickly to catch, blurring into a river of sound, and he often nodded and smiled without understanding a single thing, hoping no one would notice the confusion behind his eyes. His classmates were not unkind, but they did not know how to talk to a boy who could barely answer their simplest questions, and so, little by little, they stopped trying. Carlos spent his breaks alone by the fence, watching the others laugh at jokes he could not follow, aching with a loneliness he could not even put into words in his own language. He missed his old friends, his old streets, the easy comfort of being understood. One warm afternoon during physical education, the class divided into teams for a football match on the worn grass field. Football was the one language Carlos truly understood; he had played it on the dusty streets of his old town since before he could properly walk, chasing a ragged ball between parked cars. When the ball came to him now, something inside him woke up and roared to life. He dribbled past two startled defenders, his feet moving with a confidence his tongue had never known, and slid a perfect pass to a teammate who tapped it into the goal. For one shining, golden moment, his classmates cheered his name, even though they stumbled over the pronunciation. After the final whistle, a friendly boy named Felix jogged over, grinning broadly, and tried to say something that Carlos almost, almost understood. It was only a small beginning, just a handful of muddled words and a shared smile, but Carlos felt, for the first time since he had arrived, that the locked door might one day swing open.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Olivia loved animals more than anything in the world, and her dearest dream was to volunteer at the local animal shelter. The problem was that the shelter only accepted volunteers who were sixteen, and Olivia was just thirteen. For months she had visited the shelter after school, pressing her face against the cold wire fence to watch the dogs tumble and play in the yard, until the manager, a gruff but soft-hearted woman named Mrs Drake, finally noticed the small, patient figure who came every single day. «You can't work here yet,» Mrs Drake said, not unkindly, wiping her hands on her worn apron, «but you can help me with the small things, if your parents agree.» And so Olivia began arriving every afternoon, refilling water bowls, sweeping the kennels until they shone, and sitting quietly beside the most frightened animals until they slowly learned to trust her gentle voice. She loved every minute of it, even the tiring, smelly, unglamorous parts. Then, during one cold, rainy week, a thin, trembling dog with matted grey fur was brought in, so terrified that it pressed itself into the farthest corner of its cage and refused to eat even the softest food. The other volunteers, busy and rushed, had nearly given up on it, muttering that it was a hopeless case. But Olivia saw something in the dog's wide, frightened eyes that reminded her sharply of herself on her very first day at a new school, when everything felt too loud and too strange. Every afternoon she sat just outside the cage, talking softly, reading aloud, never forcing anything, simply waiting with all the patience she possessed. Mrs Drake watched from across the room, saying nothing, her arms folded and her face unreadable, but her sharp eyes softened a little more with each passing day.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Ben had always lived in his older sister's shadow. Grace was everything he was not: confident, popular, and effortlessly brilliant at everything she touched, from mathematics to the school choir. Teachers who had taught Grace before him often sighed when they read his name on the register and said, «I hope you're as clever as your sister.» Ben loved Grace dearly, but the constant comparison had slowly worn away at him, year after year, until he no longer believed he was truly good at anything at all. So he kept his head down, did his homework quietly in the back row, and tried his best never to be noticed by anyone. Then his school announced a community art competition, with the simple theme «My Home». Ben had never told a single soul his secret, but he loved to draw. Late at night, when the house was dark and silent and his family slept, he filled hidden sketchbooks with detailed pictures of the streets where he lived, the weathered faces of the shopkeepers, the small ordinary moments of his neighbourhood that everyone else seemed to rush past without seeing. Drawing was the one place where he felt completely free. For weeks he hesitated, certain that entering the competition was foolish, even laughable. What if he failed in front of everyone? What if people laughed, or worse, compared his clumsy work to something Grace might have done far better? The fear sat heavy in his stomach. But one quiet evening, leafing slowly through his secret drawings by the light of his bedside lamp, he felt a small, stubborn spark of pride flicker to life somewhere deep inside him. Before he could lose his nerve or change his mind, he chose his very favourite sketch, signed his name carefully at the bottom, and slipped it into the entry box the very next morning.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Aiko's grandmother was the best storyteller in the village. On warm summer evenings, the neighbours would gather on the wooden porch, fanning themselves slowly, to listen to her tales of cunning foxes, misty mountains, and clever children who outwitted lumbering giants. Her voice could rise to a roar or drop to a whisper, and the children would lean in, wide-eyed, until the stars came out. But lately, Aiko had noticed something that frightened her. Her grandmother sometimes forgot the endings, repeated the same story twice in one evening, or paused in the middle of a sentence, her hands trembling slightly as she searched for words that simply would not come. The doctor used a long, frightening name for it, but to Aiko it felt as though her grandmother were slowly slipping away, like fine sand running through open fingers no matter how tightly she tried to hold on. Aiko was only thirteen, and she did not know how to stop it, this quiet, gentle vanishing. What she did know was that her grandmother's stories were a treasure that no one had ever thought to write down. They lived only in her fading memory, passed down from her own grandmother long ago, and one day, Aiko realised with a cold ache, they would be gone forever. One quiet golden afternoon, sitting on a low stool beside her grandmother's chair, Aiko had an idea. She hurried to find an old notebook and a pen, and the next time her grandmother began a familiar tale, Aiko bent low over the page and started to write, capturing every word as fast as her hand could move. Her grandmother smiled, not quite understanding what her granddaughter was doing, and simply continued her story. Aiko wrote on, her heart aching and hopeful at once, determined to save what she still could before it was too late.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Noah had been saving money for an entire year to buy a new bicycle. He had mowed lawns all through the hot summer, walked the neighbours' dogs in the cold autumn rain, and resisted every single temptation to spend his savings on sweets, games, or comic books. Every coin felt like a small victory. By spring, he had finally collected enough, and he carried the heavy glass jar of coins and crumpled notes proudly down the street toward the bicycle shop, picturing the gleaming red bike with silver handlebars he had admired in the window for months. He could almost feel the wind in his hair already. On his way there, however, he passed the small grocery store run by old Mr Tan, a kind, gentle man who had given free fruit to the neighbourhood children for as long as Noah could remember, always with a wink and a quiet smile. A handwritten sign hung crookedly in the window, and a small, murmuring crowd had gathered on the pavement outside. Noah pushed closer and read that Mr Tan's shop had been badly damaged in a fire during the night, and that he might be forced to close it forever unless he could somehow repair the blackened shelves and replace the ruined stock. Mr Tan stood in the doorway, his shoulders bent and weary, trying his best to smile reassuringly at the worried neighbours gathered around him. Noah looked down at the heavy jar clutched tightly in his hands, the weight of a whole year's patient work, then up at the new bicycle he could already imagine himself riding triumphantly through the streets. For a long, painful moment, he stood frozen on the pavement, torn between the bright dream he had worked so hard for and the kind old man who had never, in all those years, asked anyone for anything at all.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Priya had always been afraid of water. When she was six, she had slipped into a deep pool at a birthday party and had to be hauled out, coughing, spluttering, and white with terror, by a quick-thinking lifeguard. The memory had never truly faded. Ever since that day, the mere sight of a swimming pool made her stomach twist and her palms grow damp. Now she was fifteen, and her class was setting off on a week-long trip to a wide blue lake, where swimming and canoeing filled every single afternoon on the schedule. While her friends chattered excitedly on the bus about diving contests and splashing races, Priya sat by the window in silence, secretly dreading every moment of it. She had never told anyone her shameful secret, deeply embarrassed that a girl her age still could not swim a single stroke. On the first day at the lake, she made hurried excuses, claiming a pounding headache, and watched miserably from the warm shore as the others splashed and shrieked with laughter in the cool, glittering water. The loneliness of it pressed down on her like a stone. That evening, as the sun sank low, a quiet girl named Tara, who had noticed Priya sitting alone with her arms wrapped around her knees, came and settled beside her on the warm sand. Tara did not ask any prying or awkward questions. Instead, she simply mentioned, almost casually, gazing out at the water, that she had once been terribly afraid of water too, until someone had patiently taught her, one small step at a time, in the safe, shallow end where her feet could always touch the bottom. Priya turned to look out at the calm, glittering lake, her old, familiar fear battling fiercely against a tiny, fragile new flame of hope.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The old clock tower in the town square had stopped working years ago, its iron hands frozen forever at ten past three. Most people had long since stopped noticing it, hurrying beneath it on their way to work, but for twelve-year-old Sam, the silent clock was a small, daily sadness that tugged at his heart. His late grandfather had been the town's clockmaker, a patient man with oil-stained fingers, and as a little boy Sam had often climbed the narrow, creaking wooden stairs beside him to wind the great mechanism and oil its enormous, gleaming gears. He could still remember the warm, ticking heartbeat of the tower and the proud way his grandfather had let him turn the heavy crank. After his grandfather passed away, no one in the town had the skill or the will to repair the ageing clock, and so it had simply died along with the man who had loved it best. One quiet Saturday, while exploring his grandfather's dusty old workshop, breathing in the familiar smell of oil and sawdust, Sam found a worn leather notebook tucked behind a row of jars. It was filled with careful drawings and detailed notes about the clock's inner workings. As he slowly turned the yellowed pages, his grandfather's neat, looping handwriting seemed to speak gently to him across all the years that had passed. A wild, thrilling idea suddenly took hold of him and would not let go. Perhaps, just perhaps, he could learn to fix the clock himself. He was only a boy, and he knew almost nothing about gears and springs and delicate brass wheels, but he had his grandfather's notebook, his patient memory, and a stubborn determination he was sure he had inherited from the old man. That very afternoon, Sam tucked the precious notebook firmly under his arm and made his way across the square toward the silent tower.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Lucy had won the spelling bee three years in a row, and everyone, from the youngest pupils to the proudest teachers, simply assumed she would win again. She was clever and quick, the kind of girl who could glance at a word once and lock its spelling into her memory forever, and the gleaming trophy on the assembly stage already felt like hers before the competition even began. This year, however, a shy boy named Daniel had quietly joined her class halfway through the term. Daniel rarely spoke above a whisper, and some students unkindly murmured that he was strange because he spent every single lunch break tucked away in the dim corner of the library, turning the thin pages of a fat dictionary as though it were a treasure map. Lucy, used to admiration and accustomed to winning, paid him little attention, certain that no quiet newcomer could ever threaten the crown she had worn so comfortably. The regional spelling bee arrived on a crisp autumn morning, the hall smelling of polished wood and nervous excitement, sunlight slanting through tall windows onto rows of waiting parents. One by one, the contestants stumbled and were gently eliminated, until at last only Lucy and Daniel remained on the stage, standing apart beneath the bright, humming lights. Word after difficult word, neither of them faltered, and Lucy felt, deep in her chest, the first flicker of real fear she had known in years. Then came her turn, and the announcer read out a long, twisting word she had never once heard before. Her mind went suddenly, terrifyingly blank. She glanced sideways at Daniel, fully expecting to see cold triumph shining in his eyes, but instead she saw something utterly unexpected: he was quietly mouthing the letters, one by one, gently trying to help her. Lucy stared at him, astonished, as the seconds ticked away and the whole hall waited in breathless silence for her answer.

tasks.sourceTextEn: When a sudden flood swept through the low-lying part of town, fourteen-year-old Mateo and his family were forced to abandon their home in the middle of the night, splashing through ankle-deep water with whatever they could carry. They reached the emergency shelter set up in the school gymnasium, where dozens of frightened families huddled together on thin mattresses beneath the harsh, buzzing lights that never seemed to dim. The air smelled of damp clothes and floor polish, and somewhere a baby would not stop wailing. Mateo's little sister, Lena, who was only five, cried endlessly, terrified by the strange echoing place and the worried faces of the strangers all around her. Their parents, exhausted and grey with anxiety, did their best to comfort her with soft words and tired hugs, but nothing seemed to help. Around them, other small children were crying too, and the whole gymnasium hummed with a low, restless misery that pressed on Mateo's chest like a weight. He felt small and helpless in the face of so much fear, just one boy among hundreds of shivering people. He could not stop the rising flood, rebuild the ruined houses, or bring back the warm, ordinary comfort of home and his own familiar bed. But as he watched the frightened children wiping their eyes, he suddenly remembered the silly puppet shows he used to perform to make Lena giggle, using nothing more than an old sock pulled over his hand and his own funny, exaggerated voices. A spark of an idea lit inside him. Quietly, careful not to wake the dozing adults, he began searching through the bag of belongings they had grabbed in their frantic hurry, looking for anything at all he might use. A small, determined plan was forming in his mind, and for the first time that terrible, sleepless night, Mateo felt that perhaps, in his own small way, he could do something after all.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Zoe had promised her best friend Anna that she would keep her secret, no matter what happened. Late one afternoon, her voice trembling, Anna had confided that her family was struggling badly with money, and that the reason she always wore the same scuffed, worn shoes and never once joined the others for ice cream after school was not because she was strange or stuck-up, as some classmates cruelly whispered behind her back, but simply because there was nothing at all to spare at home. Zoe had hugged her tightly, feeling Anna's thin shoulders shake, and sworn on everything she held dear never to tell a living soul. But keeping the secret grew far harder than Zoe had ever imagined. The unkind whispers about Anna spread through the class like a cold draught, and a popular, sharp-tongued girl named Bella began openly mocking Anna's shabby shoes in front of everyone, pointing and laughing. Anna pretended not to hear, staring fixedly at her desk, but Zoe saw the way her shoulders curled inward, the way her eyes shone with held-back tears she was too proud to let fall. Zoe burned with a furious mixture of anger and helplessness, her own cheeks hot. She wanted desperately to leap up and defend her friend, to shout the truth aloud and silence the cruel laughter once and for all. Yet she had given her solemn word, and revealing Anna's painful secret to the whole class would only humiliate her even more deeply, exposing the very thing Anna most wanted to hide. The two loyalties tore at her heart. One grey afternoon, as Bella began yet another round of needling, mimicking the way Anna walked, Zoe felt something inside her finally, irrevocably snap. She did not know exactly what she would do, or what words would come out of her mouth, but she knew with absolute certainty that she could not stay silent for one more second.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Every winter, the small mountain village where Kai lived was cut off by heavy snow for weeks at a time, the world shrinking to a handful of smoking chimneys and the white silence of the peaks. This year, the snow came earlier and harder than anyone could remember, falling in thick, ceaseless curtains that buried the narrow road connecting the village to the town below. At first, the villagers were not worried; they were used to long, harsh winters and had wisely kept their cupboards full of rice, dried meat, and pickled vegetables. But then old Mrs Lin, who lived alone in a small house at the edge of the village, fell seriously ill, her breathing growing shallow and her skin burning with fever, and the only doctor was on the far side of the buried mountain pass. The anxious adults gathered in Kai's family kitchen, voices low and urgent, discussing what on earth could be done, but the road was utterly impassable for cars, and a crackling phone call confirmed the worst: no rescue vehicle could reach them for at least two more days. Kai, who was fifteen and knew the twisting mountain trails better than anyone his age, listened quietly from the doorway, his heart beating faster with every grim word. He had spent every summer of his childhood hiking those steep slopes and every winter exploring the hushed, snow-laden forest until he knew its every turn. There was a hidden footpath he remembered, steep and dangerous but far shorter than the road, that he truly believed he could follow even through the deep, treacherous drifts. The grown-ups, he knew, would never in a thousand years allow it; they would forbid it the moment he spoke. But as he watched the worry carve itself deeper into every tired face around the table, Kai quietly slipped away and began to pull on his warmest clothes and his sturdiest, most reliable boots.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Isabel had spent two long years writing a story in secret, scribbling whenever she could steal a quiet moment away from the chaos of her crowded home. It was a long, fanciful tale about a girl who could talk to the wind, and into it she had poured every hope, fear, and dream she had never once dared to say aloud to another person. The story lived in a thick, worn notebook with a cracked spine that she carried everywhere and showed to no one, not even her closest, most trusted friends. Writing was the one place where Isabel felt truly free, far from the constant noise of a house crowded with loud, squabbling siblings and the relentless pressure of grades and other people's expectations. There, on the page, she was entirely herself. Then, one terrible afternoon, she opened her bag and discovered, with a lurch of pure horror, that her notebook was gone. She searched her schoolbag twice, then her locker, then her desk, her fingers trembling and her breath coming faster, growing more frantic with every passing minute. Finally, she pieced together the awful, undeniable truth: she had left it on the seat of the bus, and the bus had long since rumbled away into the afternoon traffic. Isabel felt as though a living part of her own heart had been torn out and lost somewhere on the cold city streets. Two whole years of work, of secret joy and quiet courage, vanished in a single careless instant. She wanted to cry, to give up writing forever, to believe bitterly that all her effort had been for absolutely nothing. But that evening, as she sat staring blankly at the bare wood of her empty desk, a small, stubborn voice deep inside her whispered that the story was not truly gone, because every word of it still lived somewhere safe and warm within her.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Grace had always dreamed of seeing the ocean. Growing up in a dry, dusty inland town where the summer air shimmered with heat, she had only ever glimpsed the sea in photographs and films, and over the years she had covered an entire wall of her small bedroom with pictures of rolling blue waves and wide golden beaches. Together they had planned the trip for her thirteenth birthday, saving coins in an old glass jar carefully labelled «ocean» and circling the precious date on the kitchen calendar in bright red ink. Grace had counted down the weeks with growing joy. But two months before they were due to leave, her grandfather suffered a sudden stroke that left him weak and confined to his narrow bed. The journey was quietly cancelled, and the jar of coins sat untouched and gathering dust on the shelf, a small, sad reminder of the dream they had so lovingly shared. Grace spent her long afternoons sitting faithfully beside her grandfather's bed, reading his favourite adventure books aloud and holding his thin, frail hand in both of hers. He could no longer speak clearly, his words slow and tangled, but his pale eyes still lit up like a boy's whenever she described the ocean she herself had never seen, the crash of the surf, the cry of the gulls, the endless blue horizon. One quiet evening, as the lamplight glowed softly and she watched a gentle, tender smile flicker across his tired face, Grace was suddenly struck by an idea, warm and certain, a way to bring the whole vast ocean to him, since he could no longer journey out to it.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Owen was terrified of public speaking, and now, much against his will, he had been elected as the representative for his entire class. Owen cared deeply, painfully, about the issue, far more than his cheerful nominators had guessed; his own little brother had badly cut his knee on the rusty, splintered old equipment only weeks before. But the mere thought of standing before five hundred staring students and stern-faced teachers made his mouth go bone-dry and his hands shake uncontrollably. For days he avoided so much as thinking about it, half-hoping that the assembly might somehow be quietly cancelled, that some miracle might save him. The night before, lying awake and unable to sleep, he finally sat down at his cluttered desk and tried to write his speech, but the words came out clumsy, stiff, and weak, scratched out as soon as they were written. He seriously considered begging someone braver to read it aloud for him, or simply pretending to be sick the next morning and hiding under the covers. Yet every time he closed his eyes in the darkness, he pictured the laughing younger children clambering over that dangerous, broken equipment, and he saw, clear as day, his own brother's small bandaged knee and the brave way he had tried not to cry. By the time the grey morning light crept through his curtains, Owen still had no earthly idea how he would summon the courage to speak, but he knew, with a quiet and stubborn certainty, that he simply could not let the children down.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Lily had never liked her freckles. For as long as she could remember, she had wished them away, scrubbing fiercely at her cheeks in front of the cold bathroom mirror as if she might somehow wash them off, and hiding her face behind the long curtain of her hair. A few unkind classmates had once teased her mercilessly about them back in primary school, calling her names that still made her wince, and even now the memory stung like a fresh scratch. So, at thirteen, she had grown quiet and self-conscious, always trying to make herself smaller and less noticeable, slipping through the school corridors with her head bowed, hoping no one would look at her too closely. Then a new art teacher, Ms Rivera, arrived at the school, bright-eyed and full of energy, and announced a special project that made Lily's heart sink to her shoes: each student would paint a careful self-portrait, capturing not just their outward appearance but something true and honest about who they really were inside. The very last thing on earth Lily wanted was to spend long weeks staring directly at her own freckled face. While the other students chattered excitedly about colours and brushes, she sat frozen at her desk, filled with a heavy, sinking dread. But Ms Rivera, walking slowly among the rows of desks with her hands clasped behind her back, paused beside Lily and studied her for a long, thoughtful moment, her head tilted gently to one side. «You know,» she said softly, leaning down so that only Lily could possibly hear, «I have always thought that freckles look like tiny stars scattered across the evening sky.» Then she smiled warmly and moved on to the next desk, leaving Lily sitting very still, turning the strange and wonderful new idea over and over in her mind, beginning to see herself, perhaps for the very first time, in a way she never had before.

One rainy morning, she saw him sitting there alone, soaked through, water dripping from the brim of his hat, with no umbrella, still patiently feeding the birds that huddled at his feet. Without quite thinking, Hana stopped and held her own umbrella over him, the rain drumming softly on the stretched fabric above them both. He looked up, startled, and for the first time she saw a flicker of warmth thaw the tiredness in his eyes. After that, she began to stop each morning, just for a few minutes, to wish him good morning and share a word or two about the weather or the greedy fat pigeon that always pushed the others aside. Slowly, the old man began to talk. His name was Mr Aoki, and he told her, in a quiet, halting voice that often trailed into silence, that his wife had passed away the previous year after fifty years of marriage, and that the pigeons were now his only company in the long, echoing hours. Hana listened, her heart aching for him. She was only fourteen, with little money and little power, but she found herself wondering whether there was some small way she could ease the loneliness of this gentle, forgotten old man.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Eli had always been small for his age, the shortest boy in every class photograph, the one squeezed onto the front row, the one picked last for every team while the others avoided his eyes. He had learned to laugh along when the others joked about his height, to make the joke himself before anyone else could, but inside, the constant teasing left a quiet, hollow ache that no laughter ever quite filled. This year, the school announced its annual cross-country race, a gruelling five-kilometre run across slippery hills and churned muddy fields that most students dreaded for weeks beforehand. The bigger, stronger boys boasted loudly about how easily they would win, comparing their long strides, and barely noticed Eli quietly signing his name at the bottom of the list. «You'll never finish,» one of them laughed, glancing down at him. «Your legs are too short.» Eli said nothing, his cheeks warm, but the words lit a small, stubborn fire deep inside him that refused to go out. For weeks, in secret, he trained every morning before dawn, slipping out while the house still slept, running the steep, rutted path behind his house again and again until his lungs burned and his short legs ached and trembled beneath him. He knew, with clear and honest eyes, that he would never be the fastest; that was simply impossible, no matter how hard he tried. But he made himself a private promise, whispered to the grey morning sky: he would finish the race, no matter what, and he would never give up, no matter how far behind the others he fell. On the morning of the race, Eli stood at the crowded starting line among the taller boys, his heart pounding against his ribs, holding tightly to the only thing that was truly and entirely his: his determination.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Nadia had found the wallet lying on the rain-darkened pavement outside the train station, fat with cash and cards, half-hidden beside a puddle. For a long, dizzying moment, she simply stared down at it, the crowd flowing past her on either side. Her family was poor; her mother counted every coin twice at the market, sighing over prices, and there were so many things they could not afford. The money tucked inside that wallet could pay for the warm winter coat Nadia desperately needed, the school trip she had quietly given up on while her friends chattered about it, perhaps even a small gift to bring a smile to her tired, overworked mother's face. No one had seen her bend down and pick it up. No one would ever know. She could simply slip it into her bag and walk away into the crowd, and her difficult life would suddenly become a little easier. Nadia's heart pounded against her ribs as she opened the wallet with trembling fingers and saw a photograph tucked carefully behind the worn plastic: a smiling young woman holding a small, laughing child, and beside it, a card printed with a name and an address. Suddenly the wallet was no longer just money, no longer an answer to her problems; it belonged to a real person, perhaps a mother who also worried about coins at the market, who also had a child to feed and clothe and love. Nadia stood frozen on the busy pavement, the cold autumn wind tugging at her thin, fraying jacket, torn between the powerful temptation of so much money and the quiet, insistent voice of her own conscience whispering what she already knew, deep down, was right.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The drama club's biggest production of the year was only a week away, and disaster had struck. A burst pipe had flooded the storage room overnight, soaking and ruining all the carefully made costumes and props the students had spent months stitching, gluing, and painting after school. The teacher in charge, Mr Davies, looked utterly defeated as he stood in the doorway surveying the soaked, ruined heaps, his shoulders sagging beneath the weight of it. Some students wanted to cancel the show entirely, their faces pale with disappointment; others argued bitterly that there was no possible way to make everything again in a single week. The small room filled with raised, frustrated voices. In the middle of the gloom stood Chloe, a quiet girl who had always preferred working backstage to standing in the bright glare of the spotlight, who knew every seam and prop they had lost. While the others despaired around her, her mind was already racing ahead. She thought of the flattened cardboard boxes stacked behind the supermarket, free for the taking, the heavy old velvet curtains gathering dust in her grandmother's attic, the tin of buttons and tangle of ribbons in her mother's sewing basket. Perhaps, she thought slowly, they did not need expensive costumes and flawless store-bought props at all. Perhaps, with a little imagination and the help of everyone working together, side by side, they could create something even more wonderful, more truly their own, out of almost nothing. But when she looked around at the discouraged, downcast faces of her classmates, she realised that the hardest task of all would not be making new costumes from scraps, but convincing everyone not to give up before they had even begun. Taking a deep, steadying breath, Chloe stepped forward to speak.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Jamal had been blind since birth, but he had never let it stop him from loving music with his whole heart. He could play the piano beautifully, his fingers finding the smooth keys as easily and surely as other people found words, drawing out melodies that seemed to rise from somewhere deep inside him, and he dreamed of one day joining the school orchestra. When he heard that the orchestra was holding auditions, his heart leapt with excitement and fear in equal measure. He had practised for months, learning his piece entirely by heart, every note, every rest, every gentle pause living in his memory like a path he could walk in the dark. But on the day he proudly announced his plan, he overheard two older students whispering doubtfully near the lockers. How could a blind boy possibly follow a conductor he could not see, they wondered aloud, or keep time with an entire orchestra of players around him? The careless words stung sharply, planting a cold seed of doubt in Jamal's mind that he could not shake loose. Perhaps they were right. Perhaps the bright, crowded world of the orchestra was simply not made for someone like him, and he had been foolish to dream otherwise. That evening, sitting alone at the piano in the empty house, his fingers resting silently on the cool, familiar keys, Jamal felt the old, heavy weight of other people's low expectations pressing down upon his shoulders, the weight he had carried all his life. But beneath the doubt, something stronger and warmer stirred: the memory of how music made him feel, whole and free and entirely, gloriously himself. The audition was tomorrow morning, and Jamal had a decision to make.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The summer Ada turned fourteen, her parents sent her to stay with her aunt in a quiet seaside town, far from her friends and her familiar, bustling city life. Ada was furious. She had planned a whole glorious summer of cinema trips and sleepovers and lazy afternoons, and now she was stuck in a sleepy little place where, she was utterly certain, absolutely nothing ever happened and never would. Her aunt, a calm, unhurried woman who ran a small bookshop tucked beside the harbour, did not seem in the least bothered by Ada's sulking and sighing. She simply handed her a list of small jobs to do in the shop and left her to get on with them. For the first few days, Ada dragged her feet and her temper, dusting shelves and sorting books with a sour expression, listening resentfully to the cry of the gulls and the clink of boats outside. But the bookshop was full of curious old volumes with cracked spines, faded sea charts, brittle maps, and strange forgotten treasures, and slowly, almost against her own stubborn will, Ada found herself growing curious despite everything. One grey, rainy afternoon, while sorting through a dusty box of donated books, she discovered an old, water-stained journal tucked secretly inside the hollowed cover of a thick novel. Its yellowed pages were filled with elegant, faded handwriting and what looked unmistakably like a hand-drawn map of the very town she was staying in, every street and cove carefully marked. At the bottom of the first page, in ink long since turned brown, someone had written a single mysterious line that made Ada's heart suddenly beat faster, and all at once her boring summer did not seem quite so boring after all.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Felix had a stammer, and speaking in front of others was the thing he feared most in the world. Ever since he was small, words that flowed easily and smoothly inside his mind would catch and stumble the moment they reached his tongue, breaking apart into broken syllables that made his face flush hot. Over the years he had learned a kind of survival: to stay silent rather than risk the impatient looks, the people who finished his sentences for him, the awkward pause that fell over a room while everyone waited. He spoke little, kept to the back of every class, and told himself it was safer that way. So when his history teacher cheerfully announced that every single student would have to present a project to the whole class, Felix felt sick with dread, a cold knot tightening in his stomach. He chose his topic carefully: the life of a famous explorer who had crossed frozen oceans and endless deserts, overcoming enormous hardships that would have crushed most people. For weeks he prepared, reading late into the night and rehearsing alone in the safety of his bedroom, where, strangely, his stammer always melted away and his voice rang out clear and certain. But he knew, deep down, that the crowded classroom would be a different world entirely. On the morning of his presentation, Felix's hands were damp and his heart hammered against his ribs as he walked slowly to the front of the room. He could already picture the long, painful silences, the stifled snickers, the pitying glances sliding away from his face. He opened his mouth, and for one agonising moment, nothing came out at all. Several students shifted in their seats, and Felix felt his cheeks burn. But then he remembered something: the explorer he had studied had also faced impossible obstacles, and had refused to turn back. Felix took a slow, steadying breath, looked down at his trembling notes, and tried once more to begin.

tasks.sourceTextEn: When the elderly Mrs Patel moved into the empty house next door, the children on Willow Street were both curious and a little afraid. She kept to herself, rarely came out, and her curtains stayed drawn long after the morning sun had warmed the pavement. The neighbourhood rumours grew quickly, the way rumours always do: some said she was unfriendly, others that she was simply strange, and the boldest claimed she never smiled at anyone. Twelve-year-old Sami, however, noticed something the others did not. Each evening, while the street filled with the shouts of games and the bounce of footballs, Mrs Patel sat alone at her upstairs window, watching the children play below with a wistful, faraway look in her eyes, as though she were remembering something precious and long gone. One golden afternoon, Sami's football accidentally sailed high over the fence and thudded into her quiet garden. The other children dared him to knock on her door to fetch it, giggling nervously, certain she would shout at him or worse. His heart pounding so loudly he could hear it, Sami crept up the path and rang the bell. To his surprise, Mrs Patel opened the door slowly and, instead of scolding him, smiled gently and invited him in for a moment while she searched for the ball. Her small house was warm and crowded with wonders: photographs of faraway mountains and bright crowded markets, shelves lined with carved animals, painted bowls, and curious objects gathered from a lifetime of travels across the world. As she pressed the ball back into his hands, Sami glimpsed how lonely her quiet life had become, so far from the family and the adventures of her past, surrounded only by memories. Walking slowly home through the fading light, Sami could not stop thinking about the lonely old woman behind the curtains, and he wondered whether there was something, however small, that he could do.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Tara had entered the science competition with one burning goal: to win first prize and prove to her doubting classmates that she was more than just «the quiet girl who never says anything». She had heard the whispers, felt the way their eyes slid past her as though she were invisible, and the unfairness of it had lit a quiet fire in her chest. For months she had worked entirely alone on her project, a clever little device to help elderly people remember to take their medicine on time, an idea born from watching her own grandmother struggle. She had no partner, no help, and very little money, only her notebooks crammed with sketches and calculations and a stubborn, unshakeable belief in her work. As the competition day approached, she practised her presentation over and over in front of the bathroom mirror, picturing the proud moment she would lift the gleaming trophy and watch the surprise spread across her classmates' faces. But on the morning of the contest, as the nervous contestants gathered in the echoing hall, Tara noticed a younger boy named Kofi sitting alone in a corner, close to tears. His project, a careful model of a clean-water system, had been jolted and broken during the long journey there, and the delicate parts now lay scattered uselessly across his trembling hands. The other competitors hurried past, focused only on their own chances, pretending not to see. Tara felt a pang she could not ignore. She had less than an hour before the judging began, barely enough time to compose herself and steady her own nerves. She looked down at her own polished, perfect project, ready to carry her to the victory she had dreamed of for so long, and then back at Kofi's broken one and his small, hopeless face. A difficult choice rose suddenly before her, heavy and impossible to escape, and the clock on the wall was ticking.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Oscar had always dreamed of flying. As a small boy he had built paper aeroplanes by the dozen, folding and refolding them until each crease was perfect, and he had spent whole afternoons lying in the grass watching birds wheel and glide across the sky, marvelling at how effortlessly they rode the wind. He had read every book about pilots and aircraft he could find, until the words «lift» and «thrust» felt as familiar as his own name. Now, at fifteen, his school had announced a model rocket competition, and Oscar threw himself into it with all his heart and every spare hour he had. He designed his rocket with painstaking care, calculating angles and weights, sketching diagrams, building and rebuilding the slender body late into the night until his eyes ached and the house fell silent around him. His mother worried that he was spending far too much time hunched over glue and balsa wood, but Oscar simply could not stop; this was the closest he had ever come to his lifelong dream of flight. The day before the competition, he carried his finished rocket out to the wide empty field behind his house for one final test launch, the evening air cool and still around him. With trembling hands, he set it carefully upright, lit the fuse, and stepped back, holding his breath. The rocket shot up beautifully, climbing higher and higher into the bright golden sky, and Oscar's heart soared along with it, pure joy flooding through him. But then, at the very peak of its glorious flight, something went terribly wrong. The rocket wobbled, tilted sharply, and came crashing back down, smashing into pieces on the hard, unforgiving ground. Oscar stood alone in the empty field, staring at the broken fragments of weeks of work, with the competition only a single day away and no time at all to build another.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Bella and Rosa had been best friends since they were five years old, inseparable through every grade, sharing secrets, lunches, dreams, and a thousand small jokes that no one else understood. They had built forts in Bella's garden, stayed up whispering during sleepovers, and promised each other, with the fierce solemnity of children, that they would be friends forever. But this year something had quietly changed. Rosa had begun spending more and more time with a new group of popular, confident girls, and Bella, who was shy and uncertain of herself, increasingly felt left behind, like a forgotten coat hanging by the door. The two friends still walked to school together out of long habit, but their once-easy conversation had grown awkward and thin, full of silences that never used to exist. Then came the day of the class election. Both girls, without ever telling each other, decided to run for class president. When Bella saw Rosa's name posted neatly beside her own on the candidate list, her stomach twisted with a confusing, painful mix of hurt, surprise, and rivalry. The campaign that followed was tense and uncomfortable in a way she had never imagined. Their classmates took sides, splitting into whispering groups, and Bella began to hear troubling rumours that Rosa had said unkind things about her behind her back, while Rosa, in turn, heard the very same about Bella. Years of precious friendship seemed to be dissolving, day by day, over a single foolish election. On the cold morning of the vote, Bella sat alone at her desk, remembering all the long, happy years she and Rosa had shared, every laugh and every secret, and wondering whether winning the election could ever truly be worth losing her oldest and dearest friend. She had a difficult choice to make, and very little time left in which to make it.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Diego loved to cook, but in his family, cooking was considered a girl's job, and his older brothers teased him mercilessly whenever they caught him in the kitchen, flicking dish towels at him and laughing until his ears burned. Still, Diego could not help himself, no matter how hard he tried. The kitchen was the one place in the whole world where he felt truly happy and truly himself, where the sizzle of onions in hot oil and the green smell of fresh herbs filled him with a quiet, glowing joy that nothing else could match. His grandmother, before she passed away the previous winter, had been the only one who ever understood him, patiently teaching him the family recipes and telling him, in her soft voice, that good food was a gift of love, a way of caring for the people you treasured. So when the local community centre announced a cooking competition open to all young people in the neighbourhood, Diego secretly signed his name on the list, telling no one in his family a single word. For weeks he practised in private, slipping into the kitchen whenever the house was empty, perfecting his grandmother's special stew, the one rich, fragrant dish that always made him feel close to her again, as though she were standing just behind his shoulder. But as the day of the competition drew steadily near, Diego faced a frightening prospect that kept him awake at night: to compete, he would have to cook in front of a public crowd, and there was every chance that his father and his brothers would finally discover the secret passion he had hidden away for so long.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Every autumn, the migrating cranes stopped to rest on the wide wetland near Lin's village, filling the pale sky with their great white wings and their lonely, achingly beautiful calls that echoed across the water at dawn. Lin's grandmother had taught her to love the birds when she was very small, lifting her onto her shoulders to watch them land, and ever since, each year Lin counted the days until their return as though waiting for old friends. But this year, fewer cranes came than ever before, and the empty stretches of sky made her chest tighten with worry. The wetland had been shrinking season by season, drained little by little to make room for new farmland, and the shallow water that the cranes depended on grew muddy, thin, and choked with weeds. One cold, misty morning, Lin found a young crane tangled helplessly in a discarded fishing net at the water's edge, its slender wing trapped, its dark eyes wide with terror. She freed it gently, her hands shaking with cold and fear, murmuring soft words to calm it, and carried it carefully home to nurse it back to health. As she cared for the injured bird over the following days, feeding it and watching its strength slowly return, a troubling thought grew and deepened in her mind. Saving this one crane was not enough; if the wetland continued to disappear, the cranes might one day stop coming altogether, and a tradition older even than her grandmother would be lost forever, vanishing like mist in the morning sun. Lin was only thirteen, with no money and no power and no one important to speak for her. But as she looked into the recovering crane's dark, trusting eyes, she felt a fierce, unshakeable determination rise within her to protect not just this one fragile bird, but all the cranes, and the precious wetland they called home.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Henry had broken his leg in a cycling accident just two weeks before the school's big mountain hike, the one event he had been looking forward to all year long. He had circled the date on his calendar months in advance and pictured himself standing triumphantly at the summit. Now, while his friends excitedly packed their backpacks and broke in their new boots, Henry sat at home with his leg in a heavy plaster cast, watching the bright world go on without him through the living-room window. He felt useless and forgotten, utterly certain that the whole grand adventure would happen far away on the mountainside and that he would simply be left behind, alone and pointless. His friends had visited often at first, signing his cast and cheering him up, but as the hike drew closer they grew busy with their preparations, and Henry's frustration slowly curdled into a lonely, sour bitterness he could not shake. Then his closest friend, Marcus, came by one quiet evening with an unexpected idea shining in his eyes. The teachers, Marcus explained eagerly, still needed someone reliable to coordinate the entire hike from the base camp: tracking each group's progress, managing the supplies, and keeping everyone safe by radio through the long day. It was an important job, perhaps the most important one of all, Marcus insisted, and best of all, it could be done sitting comfortably down. At first, Henry only scowled and turned his face away, certain it was just a kind way of making him feel included out of pity. He did not want a consolation prize handed to him gently; he wanted to climb the mountain with his own two legs, like everyone else. But as Marcus went on describing the real responsibility involved, the trust the teachers would be placing in him, Henry felt the first faint, surprising stirring of a new and different kind of excitement.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Yuki had always been told she was «too sensitive». She cried easily at sad films, long after the lights came up, and she felt other people's pain so vividly that it seemed to become her own, settling in her chest like a stone. The noise and rush of the busy world often overwhelmed her, leaving her exhausted by the smallest things. Her classmates sometimes laughed gently at how deeply she felt everything, and over the years Yuki had slowly grown to see her sensitivity as a shameful weakness, a fault she desperately wished she could simply switch off like a light. Then her school announced a special project: every student was to spend a whole month volunteering somewhere in the community, giving their time to others. Yuki was assigned to a home for elderly people, and she arrived on the first day nervous and unsure, clutching her bag tightly, afraid that her shyness and her tender heart would only get in the way and embarrass her. But something quite unexpected happened. The elderly residents, many of them lonely and rarely visited by anyone at all, responded warmly to Yuki's gentle, patient attention, their faces brightening when she entered the room. She noticed the small, quiet things that the others missed entirely: who needed a hand to hold during a difficult afternoon, who longed simply to be truly listened to, who carried hidden sorrows behind a brave, cheerful face. Where the other volunteers hurried briskly through their tasks, eager to finish, Yuki lingered, truly seeing the people in front of her, hearing the stories they had waited so long to tell. By the end of her very first week, watching an old woman's eyes fill with grateful tears, she began to wonder whether the very sensitivity she had always considered a flaw might in fact be something far more valuable than she had ever realised.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The old lighthouse on the cliff had not been lit in over thirty years, ever since a sleek modern beacon had been built further along the rocky coast. To most people in the town, it was just a crumbling, weather-beaten relic, its paint peeling and its windows dark. But to twelve-year-old Maris, it was the most magical place in the whole world. Her great-grandfather had once been its faithful keeper, climbing its winding stairs every single night, and the family still kept his worn leather logbooks, their pages filled with stories of howling storms, desperate ships, and lights guiding weary sailors safely home through the dark. Maris spent long, happy hours exploring the abandoned tower, running her fingers along the cold iron railings and imagining the great lamp blazing gloriously once more, sweeping its beam across the black waves. Then one day she learned the terrible news: the town council planned to tear the lighthouse down, calling it a useless danger that cost far too much to maintain and served no purpose anymore. A wave of sadness and burning anger swept over Maris all at once. The lighthouse was part of the town's history, part of her own family's story, woven into who she was, and once it was gone, it would be gone forever, lost like a ship swallowed by the sea. The council meeting where the final, irreversible decision would be made was only two short weeks away. Maris was just a child, she reminded herself bitterly; no one important would ever listen to her, no one would take a twelve-year-old seriously. And yet, as she stood at the foot of the silent tower, gazing up at its proud, weathered stones glowing softly in the afternoon light, she felt with sudden certainty that she could not simply stand by and watch it disappear without at least trying, with all her heart, to save it.
